general_suggestions: get air conditioning in the barn. It was very hot even with fans.

course_suggestions: This project was overly ambitious for the time allotted for the class. Instead of this being a fun interactive class, it felt like we were on a 10 hr shift at a basket making sweat shop. The instructor seemed to assume people already knew how to weave baskets which is weird since this was a class to learn how to weave baskets. She was also rather insensitive to my mother who was having trouble hearing and getting around the room. I'd be happy to discuss this further with you if you are interested in hearing more.
general_suggestions: Yes! I really want the folk school to be a success! This was an excellent idea and I really hope it continues to grow. That being said, I was surprised to see the state of the facility where this class was located. It has great potential but the bathrooms were dirty and smelled of sewage, the bathroom trash cans were full, and the floors upstairs did not appear to have been swept for quite some time. The elevator was a bit scary, mostly because no one seemed to know how to use it.

additional_learning: I'd love to learn some more technique with the other carving tips, and the technique Felix mentioned about carving further away from the block to make multi layer prints.
course_suggestions: I would make the class an hour longer, but build in a 15 minute break, where I'd have the instructor lead the class in stretching out their hands a bit. I know mine were sore from all the tracing, before we even got to carving.
general_suggestions: Maybe have the cafe open when classes will be, so people can get dinner there for class.

additional_learning: Mainly I would like to gain more experience with the techniques I learned in class and experiment with them a bit, but in the future I would be interested in more complex techniques (like printing in multiple colors)
course_suggestions: It was really good, Felix knows what they're doing and is a great teacher (and artist)
general_suggestions: Allerton is such a beautiful place -- maybe classes can be held in rooms that are not so ugly, and that are more suited to the subject (i.e., it would have been nice(r) to have a sink in the room) -- maybe other classes are already held in more aesthetically-pleasing locations?

general_suggestions: We were a bit disappointed in the class. While we did DISCUSS "how to make simple yet elegant appetizers", what I was envisioning is that we would actually make several complete appetizers. Instead, what we did was sort of make a part of an appetizer. There were 4 appetizers discussed, but the only thing we physically did was make the phyllo dough shells for part of an appetizer, no filling, and then there were only 3 pans to cook them, leaving several participants to wait so long their shells dried out and crumbled, they shared their disappointment on the way out. We did make a pepperoni rose, which admittedly was nice to learn. I bought this as a surprise gift, and it kind of fell flat. At $60 each, we expected more of an experience, not just a discombobulated chat. The instructor was very kind and clearly new a lot, but it seemed she wasn't really prepared for the class. For example, there were not enough ingredients to make the olive appetizer, and she "meant to" make some kind of biscuit sandwich "but just ran out of time". We love supporting these sorts of things and love the Alerton property and events but this one fell far short of what we have come to expect.

I think intros at each table are fine, but not for the whole room to do that. I would think most people wanted to learn how to make a wreath, drink wine, and chat with their friends. I also think for the cost of the class, we should've been able to use more than three decorative items on our wreaths. Five would've been more ideal. The items were secondhand (which is great) with plenty left over, so I feel like that would be reasonable.

additional_learning: This might be a pipe dream but it would be so cool to have an overnight backpacking 101 class where we learn leave no trace principles, gain some experience hiking with our gear, low-impact camp set-up, and some basic orienteering/navigation skills. Allerton would be perfect because it is quiet and scenic (and secure) and doing a loop hike that returns to camp near the main buildings & facilities to minimize impact to the more delicate natural areas. I think this could be done in a way that is as low impact as the foraging or winter botany classes. I know there's already some camping that happens in the meadow from time to time, so similar to that but with more hiking, navigation, and skill-building. If that makes sense.

general_suggestions: Jeanette is a delightful, wonderful person who made class fun. However, we had to stop attending after the first class. My husband and I - as well as all attendees - had no warning that the class would be 2 hours of high impact aerobics or that there could be a danger of hurting our ankles or knees. It's a heart attack waiting to happen. There were so many older couples. The class was way too long and too intensive. I am 52 and exercise regularly, but have to see an orthopedic surgeon for my ankle injury from just one class. It's sad, because I loved the location, the people, the history of the dances, the dances, themselves, but wish I had never gone. I've been wearing an ankle brace ever since. Not to mention that we were out the money we paid for the class. Also, an older woman fell down and at one point, and once recovered, looked like she might faint. She had to leave to go to the restroom and was gone a long time. Truly, people need a warning about the intensity. Jeannette is so kind and fun, but seemed completely unaware of the danger for these folks, especially the seniors.

satisfaction_comment: The instructor gave us 15 grams of metal clay which is enough for the intended project. And, metal clay is expensive. She had us make a slurry with about 1/2 gram of clay and had us paint the clay onto small leaves. This process is long and tedious as you should paint many layers and dry them in between. During that process, she showed us how to make molds which took 20 minutes to dry in the same oven that we were using. So, we all had to wait. When it came to the torch firing, she had a brand new torch that she didn't know how to fill. At the end of the day, I still have 14 1/2 grams of metal clay and no pendant or earrings. This class was poorly planned and the objectives were not met.
general_suggestions: If the instructor would like to teach a silver metal clay class again, I have experience and would be happy to be an assistant.
testimonial: Unfortunately, I was very disappointed with the outcome. I spent over $170, expecting to get a pendant and earrings. I was looking forward to learning from a new instructor and making some new jewelry. At this point, I need to work up my 14 1/2 grams of metal clay before it dries out. For me, I have the tools and experience. For the rest of the class, they are left with metal clay that they don't know how to use.
